comments:
Summary
This manuscript presents an extensive computational study of Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS) algorithms for the multi-depot Dial-a-Ride Problem (DARP) with heterogeneous vehicles and users. The authors extend a standard MILP formulation to the multi-depot heterogeneous case, propose/compare a number of initialization heuristics, six shaking neighborhoods, several intra-route local searches (including focused variants and an 8-step timing scheme), and an adjusting procedure for near-feasible solutions. Algorithms are evaluated on three benchmark sets: (i) classical single-depot instances from the literature, (ii) multi-depot extensions from [6], and (iii) instances derived from a real healthcare application; comparisons are made against optimal/known-best values and CPLEX where applicable.
Major strengths
- The paper tackles a practically important and challenging variant of DARP (multi-depot, heterogeneous vehicles/users, time windows, ride-time, capacity and route duration constraints), which increases its applied relevance.
- The work is methodical: multiple neighborhoods and local searches are designed and tested; the paper reports many experiments and uses real-world derived instances in addition to literature benchmarks. The release of instance data (URL referenced) is also valuable for reproducibility.
- The MILP formulation is a reasonable extension of existing models (Cordeau) to the multi-depot heterogeneous setting, and the use of an evaluation function with dynamic penalty weights is a sensible practical choice for VNS.
Major weaknesses and points for improvement
- Comparative baselines and statistical analysis: Although the authors compare to optimal/best-known values and to CPLEX where possible, the manuscript lacks comparisons with several recent state-of-the-art metaheuristics (beyond the cited works) and does not present statistical significance testing. Given stochasticity (randomized shaking, randomized initializations), reporting variance, confidence intervals, or tests (e.g., Wilcoxon) would strengthen conclusions.
- Parameter tuning and robustness: Key parameter choices (initial penalty coefficients α0, β0, γ0, τ0, the δ update strategy, neighborhood size hmax) are reported but lacking motivation or tuning methodology. A sensitivity analysis showing how results depend on these parameters would be helpful, as would guidelines for practitioners.
- Computational environment and reproducibility: The paper omits details about the hardware used, time limits per run, software versions and random seeds. While instances are provided, code/implementations are not mentioned; inclusion of code or more detailed experimental protocol would improve reproducibility.
- Algorithmic clarity: The descriptions of some components (e.g., the adjusting procedure, the rationale for the 1.02 threshold used before local search in Algorithm 1, and the choice of neighborhoods order) are brief; expanding on motivations and pseudocode/complexity would aid readers.
- Presentation issues: The provided text appears to contain formatting artifacts and repetitions (likely PDF-to-text conversion artifacts) near the end; the manuscript should be carefully proofread. Some tables are summarized in the narrative but full tables and clear captions would help readers interpret results more easily.
Additional suggestions
- Include per-instance runtimes and scaling behaviour (how solution time grows with instance size), and report exactly how many runs per instance and how random seeds were handled.
- Ablation study: report the impact of individual neighborhoods and local searches by progressively enabling/disabling them to show which components contribute most to solution quality and time.
- Justify Heuristic 2 (1000 random starts) in terms of cost/benefit compared to Heuristic 1 and consider adaptive stopping criteria.
- If possible, release implementation code or at least a detailed pseudo-code appendix to assist reproducibility.
Overall assessment
This is a solid experimental study that adapts and extends known VNS techniques to a richer and practically-motivated DARP variant and reports extensive computational results on literature and real-derived instances. The paper would benefit from stronger comparative baselines, more thorough statistical reporting, clearer description of algorithmic choices and experimental protocol, and cleanup of presentation artifacts. With these improvements, the contribution would be a valuable resource for both researchers and practitioners working on DARP variants.
